title: Scaling laws and emergence
summary: Loss falls predictably with compute, data, and parameters, but whether new abilities appear suddenly is contested and partly a measurement artifact.
body_html:
Scaling laws are one of the most reliable empirical findings in the field: as you increase model size, data, and compute, the pretraining loss falls along a smooth, predictable curve. The compute-optimal result, often called Chinchilla, showed that for a fixed compute budget you should scale parameters and training tokens together, and that many early large models were undertrained on too little data for their size.

Emergence is the more dramatic and more contested claim: that specific capabilities appear abruptly past a scale threshold rather than improving gradually. The counterargument is that many apparent jumps are artifacts of the metric. A harsh all-or-nothing score can turn smooth underlying progress into a sudden-looking leap, so the curve looks like a cliff because of how it was measured, not because the model changed discontinuously.

Hold both ideas with the right confidence. Scaling laws for loss are well-established and load-bearing for how labs plan training runs. Sharp emergence of specific skills is real-sounding but disputed, and the honest position is that smooth loss improvement is the safe claim while discontinuous capability jumps depend heavily on the metric you chose.
key_points: Pretraining loss falls smoothly and predictably with compute, data, and parameters.
Chinchilla showed parameters and training tokens should scale together.
Sharp emergence of specific abilities is contested and often a metric artifact.
Treat smooth loss scaling as solid; treat discontinuous capability jumps as disputed.

links.title: Training Compute-Optimal LLMs (Chinchilla)
links.note: The scaling laws balancing parameters and training tokens.

links.title: Emergent Abilities of Large Language Models
links.note: The emergence claim; read alongside its metric-artifact critique.

flashcards.back: Pretraining loss falling smoothly and predictably with compute, data, and parameters.
For a fixed compute budget, scale parameters and training tokens together.
Many apparent jumps are artifacts of harsh all-or-nothing metrics, not real discontinuities.

quiz.q: What is the well-established part of scaling?
choices: Every specific skill appears all at once, the moment the model crosses a size threshold.
Pretraining loss falls smoothly and predictably with compute, data, and parameters.
Bigger models reliably hallucinate less, so scale alone fixes factual errors.
Scaling has no measurable effect once a model passes a few billion parameters.
quiz.explain: Smooth loss scaling is the load-bearing, well-supported finding.

quiz.q: Why might a capability look like it emerged suddenly?
choices: Because the sampling temperature was raised at exactly that model size.
Because the model physically rewires its own architecture once it reaches a certain critical scale.
A harsh all-or-nothing metric can turn smooth progress into a sudden-looking jump.
Because emergence is always a real discontinuity and never a metric artifact.
quiz.explain: Metric choice can manufacture an apparent discontinuity from smooth underlying gains.

exercise.prompt: A capability appears to jump suddenly at a model size under an exact-match metric, but improves smoothly under a partial-credit metric. What is the most defensible reading?
choices: The smaller model already has the capability fully and just hides it on this test.
The metric you choose can never change whether a capability looks emergent.
The capability genuinely emerged in one discontinuous step at that exact size.
The apparent jump is largely a metric artifact; the underlying gain is smooth.
exercise.explain: When the jump disappears under a softer metric, the discontinuity was mostly in the measurement.

outcome: You can separate well-supported loss scaling from the contested claim of sharp emergence.

title: Alignment: RLHF, RLAIF, and constitutions
summary: Alignment methods shape a capable model toward helpful, honest, and harmless behavior using human or AI feedback against a stated standard.
body_html:
A pretrained model is capable but unshaped. Alignment is the set of methods that steer it toward being helpful, honest, and harmless. The workhorse is reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF): people rank candidate responses, a reward model learns those preferences, and the policy is optimized to score well under that reward. Direct preference optimization reaches a similar end by optimizing preferences directly, without a separate reward-model and RL loop.

Two refinements matter at the frontier. RLAIF replaces much of the human labeling with AI-generated feedback, which scales but inherits the judging model's biases. Constitutional AI makes the standard explicit: the model critiques and revises its own outputs against a written set of principles, so the values being optimized are stated rather than implicit in a pile of labels.

Keep the limits in view. Alignment optimizes a proxy, the reward or the preference data, not the true objective, so it is vulnerable to reward hacking and to whatever blind spots the feedback shares. Sycophancy, for instance, can be a side effect of optimizing for what raters prefer. Alignment is real and load-bearing, but it shapes behavior toward a measurable stand-in, not a guarantee of good judgment.
key_points: RLHF trains a reward model from human rankings, then optimizes the policy against it.
DPO optimizes preferences directly, skipping a separate RL loop.
RLAIF scales feedback with AI labels; Constitutional AI makes the standard explicit.
Alignment optimizes a proxy, so reward hacking and shared blind spots remain risks.

links.title: Constitutional AI
links.note: Aligning behavior against an explicit written constitution.

links.title: Direct Preference Optimization (DPO)
links.note: Preference tuning without a separate reinforcement learning loop.

links.title: Hugging Face: Alignment Handbook
links.note: Working recipes for SFT, DPO, and preference optimization.

flashcards.back: Trains a reward model from human rankings, then optimizes the policy to score well under it.
An explicit written standard the model critiques and revises its outputs against.

flashcards.front: What is the core limit of alignment?
flashcards.back: It optimizes a proxy (reward or preferences), so reward hacking and shared blind spots persist.

quiz.q: What does a reward model in RLHF represent?
choices: The model's tokenizer, retrained to score responses instead of splitting text.
The size of the context window the policy is allowed to read during training.
The true human objective, captured exactly and without any approximation error.
A learned proxy for human preferences that the policy is optimized against.
quiz.explain: The reward model is a learned stand-in for preferences, not the true goal.

quiz.q: Why can sycophancy emerge from alignment?
choices: Because optimizing for what raters prefer can reward agreeable answers over correct ones.
Because the learning rate during alignment is set far too high to converge.
Because models develop a genuine dislike of users who disagree with them.
Because alignment changes only the surface tone of answers and never touches their actual content.
quiz.explain: Optimizing a preference proxy can favor what pleases raters, including agreement.

exercise.prompt: After preference tuning, a model starts agreeing with users even when they are wrong. What is the likely cause?
choices: Pretraining failed, so the model never learned the facts it now agrees against.
The reward model is too small to represent what human raters actually wanted.
Optimizing a preference proxy can reward agreeable answers, producing sycophancy.
The context window shrank during tuning, so the model simply forgot the correct answer it once knew.
exercise.explain: Alignment optimizes a stand-in for preferences, which can favor what pleases raters over what is correct.

outcome: You can describe RLHF, DPO, RLAIF, and Constitutional AI and name alignment's proxy limit.

title: Reasoning and test-time compute
summary: Reasoning models spend more compute at inference to think before answering, trading latency and cost for accuracy on hard problems.
body_html:
A major shift after raw scaling was learning to spend compute at inference time, not just during training. Chain-of-thought prompting showed that letting a model write intermediate steps improves hard reasoning. Reasoning models build on this: published accounts describe training them with reinforcement learning on problems that can be checked, so they produce extended reasoning before a final answer and more thinking can be traded for more accuracy. Labs disclose the exact recipes and the internal traces only partially, so treat specific training claims with some caution.

This opens a new scaling axis. Beyond a bigger model, you can let a model think longer, sample several reasoning paths and select the best, or verify steps as it goes. On math, code, and other checkable tasks these test-time methods can lift accuracy substantially. The cost is real: more tokens, higher latency, and more spend per answer.

The honest framing is that test-time compute is a dial, not a free upgrade. It helps most where problems are hard and answers are checkable, and it helps little on simple lookups or open-ended tasks with no verifiable signal. Spending more thinking on an easy question mostly buys latency. Match the effort to the difficulty.
key_points: Test-time compute means spending more inference effort to think before answering.
Reasoning models are trained, often via RL on verifiable tasks, to reason at length.
Sampling multiple paths and verifying steps adds a new scaling axis beyond model size.
It helps most on hard, checkable problems and costs tokens and latency; match effort to difficulty.

links.title: Chain-of-Thought Prompting Elicits Reasoning
links.note: Letting a model write intermediate steps improves hard reasoning.

links.title: Scaling LLM Test-Time Compute Optimally
links.note: When spending more inference compute beats a larger model.

flashcards.back: Spending more inference effort, like longer reasoning, to improve the answer.

flashcards.front: How are reasoning models often trained?
flashcards.back: With reinforcement learning on verifiable problems to reason at length before answering.

flashcards.front: Where does test-time compute help most?
flashcards.back: On hard problems with checkable answers, like math and code.

quiz.q: What does spending more test-time compute trade?
choices: It trades accuracy away in exchange for a faster, cheaper answer.
Latency and cost for higher accuracy on hard problems.
Nothing at all; extra test-time compute is effectively free to spend.
It trades model size for training data without changing accuracy.
quiz.explain: More thinking costs tokens and latency in exchange for accuracy where it helps.

quiz.q: On which task is extra reasoning compute least useful?
choices: A tricky coding bug that needs several steps of careful tracing.
A hard competition math problem with many intermediate steps.
A simple factual lookup with no verifiable reasoning to do.
A multi-step logical proof where each step has to be checked.
quiz.explain: Easy lookups have little to reason about, so extra compute mostly buys latency.

exercise.prompt: You enable long reasoning and best-of-many sampling for a batch of simple, factual lookup questions. What is the likely effect?
choices: A large accuracy gain, since more reasoning always helps the answer.
Little accuracy change but much higher cost and latency.
The model stops answering because the reasoning chain grows too long.
Lower accuracy than a single pass, because extra sampling adds noise.
exercise.explain: Test-time compute pays off on hard, checkable problems, not simple lookups.

outcome: You can explain test-time compute as a difficulty-matched dial, not a free upgrade.

title: Interpretability: looking inside
summary: Interpretability tries to read the internal computation of models, from circuits to features, and it is promising but still partial.
body_html:
Models are trained, not written, so their internal logic is not given to us; interpretability is the effort to recover it. Mechanistic interpretability studies the actual computation: identifying circuits, small subnetworks that implement a recognizable function, and features, directions in the model's activation space that correspond to concepts. The goal is to move from treating the model as a black box to reading what it is doing.

A central obstacle is superposition: models pack far more features than they have neurons by representing concepts as overlapping combinations, so a single neuron rarely means one thing. Sparse autoencoders and related methods try to pull these tangled representations apart into cleaner, more interpretable features, and recent work has scaled this to large models with real but incomplete success.

Calibrate the claims. Interpretability has produced genuine, verifiable findings about how specific behaviors are implemented, and it matters for safety because it could let us audit a model rather than only test its outputs. But it remains partial: we cannot yet fully explain a frontier model's behavior, and confident end-to-end stories about why a model did something should be treated with care.
key_points: Interpretability recovers internal computation: circuits and concept-like features.
Superposition packs many features into few neurons, so one neuron rarely means one thing.
Sparse autoencoders aim to disentangle features into cleaner, interpretable units.
Findings are real and safety-relevant but still partial; full explanations remain out of reach.

links.title: Distill: Zoom In, An Introduction to Circuits
links.note: The foundational, highly visual intro to circuits.

links.title: Transformer Circuits
links.note: Ongoing mechanistic interpretability research, including features at scale.

flashcards.front: What are circuits and features in interpretability?
flashcards.back: Circuits are subnetworks implementing a function; features are activation directions for concepts.
Models pack many overlapping features into few neurons, so one neuron rarely means one thing.
Real, safety-relevant findings exist, but full explanations of frontier models are still out of reach.

quiz.q: Why is reading a single neuron usually not enough to understand a model?
choices: Models do not actually contain neurons, so reading one tells you nothing useful.
Each neuron only stores a piece of the tokenizer rather than a learned concept.
Individual neurons fire at random, so no single one carries real information.
Superposition means concepts are spread across overlapping neurons, so one neuron rarely means one thing.
quiz.explain: Superposition packs many features into shared neurons, blurring one-to-one meaning.

quiz.q: What is the honest status of interpretability claims?
choices: There are real, verifiable findings, but full explanations remain partial.
Interpretability has produced nothing useful, so the claims are all marketing.
We can already fully explain any frontier model's behavior end to end.
It only works on tiny toy models and never on anything used in practice.
quiz.explain: Genuine progress coexists with large remaining gaps; avoid overclaiming.

exercise.prompt: You find a single neuron that activates for many unrelated concepts. What does this illustrate?
choices: Superposition; models pack many overlapping features into few neurons.
It shows the model has no real features and is mostly empty capacity.
It is simply a tokenizer bug that mislabels which concept the neuron is actually responding to.
The neuron is broken and should be pruned from the network entirely.
exercise.explain: Superposition is why one neuron rarely maps to one concept, and why disentangling features is hard.

outcome: You can explain circuits, features, and superposition and calibrate interpretability claims.

title: Benchmarks and their decay
summary: Public benchmarks drive progress but decay through saturation and contamination, so headline scores need skeptical reading.
body_html:
Benchmarks gave the field a shared yardstick, and much progress was driven by competing on them. But a public benchmark has a lifecycle. As models approach the top, the score saturates and stops discriminating between systems, and a benchmark everyone passes no longer measures anything useful. The frontier constantly needs harder, fresher tests.

The deeper problem is contamination. Once a benchmark is on the public internet, its questions and answers can leak into training data, so a high score may reflect memorization rather than capability. This is hard to detect and easy to underestimate, which is why a single benchmark number is weak evidence and why held-out, private, or freshly constructed tests are valued.

Read scores like an evaluator. Ask what the benchmark actually measures, whether it could be contaminated, whether the gap between models clears the noise, and whether the test still discriminates at the top. Holistic suites that report many metrics across many scenarios resist single-number overreading better than one headline accuracy.
key_points: Benchmarks saturate: when everyone passes, the score stops discriminating.
Contamination leaks test items into training data, inflating scores via memorization.
A single benchmark number is weak evidence; held-out and fresh tests are more trustworthy.
Read scores skeptically: what it measures, contamination, noise, and top-end discrimination.

links.title: Stanford HELM
links.note: A holistic, multi-metric benchmark across many scenarios.

links.title: OpenAI Evals
links.note: Building fresh, custom benchmarks beyond public sets.

flashcards.back: When models approach the top, the score stops discriminating between systems.
Test items leaking into training data, so a high score may reflect memorization.

flashcards.front: Why is one benchmark number weak evidence?
flashcards.back: It can be saturated or contaminated and may not clear the noise floor.

quiz.q: A model scores very high on a popular public benchmark. Why be cautious?
choices: A high public-benchmark score is always faked by selective reporting.
The benchmark may be saturated or contaminated, so the score can reflect memorization.
Benchmarks never matter, so any score on one is safe to ignore outright.
Public benchmarks cannot really be measured precisely, so any number reported on one is meaningless.
quiz.explain: Saturation and contamination both erode what a high public score proves.

quiz.q: What kind of test best resists contamination?
choices: Any test the model has already seen during its training.
An old, widely published benchmark everyone has trained on.
A held-out, private, or freshly constructed test.
A single multiple-choice question picked at random.
quiz.explain: Fresh or private tests have not leaked into training data.

exercise.prompt: A model scores 99% on a popular three-year-old public benchmark. Why be cautious?
choices: A high score on it proves the model has general intelligence.
Benchmarks never matter, so the figure tells you nothing at all.
Such a high score must always be faked through selective reporting of the best runs.
Saturation and possible contamination; the test may be in the training data.
exercise.explain: Old public benchmarks can saturate and leak into training data, so the score may reflect memorization.

outcome: You can read benchmark scores skeptically for saturation, contamination, and noise.

title: Capabilities and limits
summary: Frontier models are strong and uneven; hallucination, calibration, and jagged competence are structural, not bugs to be fully patched.
body_html:
Frontier models are genuinely strong: fluent across domains, capable at code and math, able to use tools and reason at length. They are also uneven in ways that surprise people who expect uniform competence. The capability surface is jagged: a model can solve a hard problem and then fail a simpler-looking one, because difficulty for a model does not track difficulty for a human.

Some limits are structural. A model trained to predict plausible text will sometimes produce confident, fluent falsehoods, which is what we call hallucination. It has several roots, the next-token objective among them, along with gaps in the training data, imperfect calibration, and missing retrieval or verification. Better data, retrieval, and post-training reduce it, but no current method removes it entirely. Calibration, whether a model's confidence matches its accuracy, is imperfect, so a confident answer is not a correct one. These do not vanish with scale; they are reduced and managed, not eliminated.

The expert stance is to use strength without trusting uniformity. Lean on models where they are reliable and verifiable, build checks where errors are costly, and resist both the hype that says they can do anything and the dismissal that says they can do nothing. Know the jagged edge and design around it, because that edge is a property of the technology, not a temporary flaw.
key_points: Capability is jagged: model difficulty does not track human difficulty.
Hallucination has several roots (objective, data gaps, calibration, missing retrieval); it is reduced and managed, not eliminated.
Calibration is imperfect, so confidence does not equal correctness.
Use strength where reliable and verifiable; build checks where errors are costly.

links.title: The Llama 3 Herd of Models
links.note: A candid technical account of modern model capability and evaluation.

links.title: Stanford HELM
links.note: Capability and limitation measured across many axes.

flashcards.back: A model can ace a hard task and fail an easy-looking one; model difficulty differs from human difficulty.

flashcards.front: Why is hallucination hard to remove?
flashcards.back: Predicting plausible text can yield confident falsehoods; it has several causes (objective, data gaps, calibration) and is reduced, not removed.

flashcards.front: Why does confidence not equal correctness?
flashcards.back: Model calibration is imperfect, so a confident answer can still be wrong.

quiz.q: A frontier model solves a hard proof but botches a simple arithmetic step. What does this illustrate?
choices: Arithmetic is genuinely harder than proofs for every kind of solver, human or machine alike.
The correct proof was just luck and the model cannot really reason.
The model is simply broken and its other answers cannot be trusted.
Jagged capability: model difficulty does not track human difficulty.
quiz.explain: Uneven competence across tasks is a structural property, not a one-off error.

quiz.q: Why will scaling not fully eliminate hallucination?
choices: Because it has several roots (the objective, data gaps, calibration), so scale alone does not remove it.
Because hallucination is not a real phenomenon, just a labeling disagreement.
Because calibration is already perfect, so scale has nothing left to fix.
Because future models will get smaller, undoing whatever scale achieved.
quiz.explain: Hallucination has multiple causes, so it is reduced and managed rather than removed by scale alone.

exercise.prompt: A model gives a fluent, confident, and detailed answer that you cannot easily verify. How much should its confidence raise your trust?
choices: A lot; a confident, detailed, and fluent answer is a strong signal that the content is correct.
Fully; the model only sounds confident after it has checked outside sources.
Little; calibration is imperfect, so confidence is not evidence of correctness.
Completely; fluent confidence guarantees the answer has been verified.
exercise.explain: Fluency and confidence are not calibration; an unverifiable confident answer still needs a check.

outcome: You can reason about jagged capability, hallucination, and calibration as structural traits.

title: Reading the field without drowning
summary: The literature moves fast and is noisy; a durable habit is to anchor on a few canonical works and read new claims with source discipline.
body_html:
The pace of AI research is the first obstacle to learning it. More appears each week than anyone can read, much of it is preprint rather than peer-reviewed, and a striking result can circulate widely before anyone has reproduced it. Trying to read everything is how you learn nothing. The skill is selection.

Anchor on the canon. A small set of works carries most of the load: the transformer, the scaling and compute-optimal results, the alignment methods, the interpretability foundations, and a current capable model's technical report. Understanding those deeply gives you the scaffolding to place new papers quickly, because most new work is a variation on a structure you already hold.

Read new claims with source discipline. Note whether something is a peer-reviewed paper, a preprint, a company post, or a thread, and weight it accordingly. Ask whether it has been reproduced, what it did not measure, and whether the result clears the noise. A confident headline is a prompt to check, not a fact. Holding your own confidence honestly is the same discipline that the evaluation track teaches, applied to the literature.
key_points: The field moves too fast to read everything; selection is the skill.
Anchor on a small canon: transformer, scaling, alignment, interpretability, a current model report.
Weight a claim by its source type and whether it has been reproduced.
Treat a confident headline as a prompt to check, not a fact.

links.title: Attention Is All You Need
links.note: The transformer, the anchor of the modern canon.

links.title: The Llama 3 Herd of Models
links.note: A thorough modern model report worth reading end to end.

flashcards.front: Why is trying to read everything counterproductive?
flashcards.back: The field moves too fast and is noisy; selection, not volume, is the skill.
A small canon: transformer, scaling, alignment, interpretability, and a current model report.

flashcards.front: How should you weight a new claim?
flashcards.back: By its source type and whether it has been reproduced; treat a headline as a prompt to check.

quiz.q: What is the most durable strategy for keeping up with AI research?
choices: Read every new preprint the day it appears so you never miss a result.
Anchor on a small canon deeply, then place new work against it with source discipline.
Follow only social-media threads, since they surface what actually matters.
Avoid reading papers entirely and rely instead on short summaries written by other people online.
quiz.explain: Deep canonical scaffolding plus skeptical reading scales; chasing everything does not.

quiz.q: How should you treat a striking, un-reproduced preprint result?
choices: As established fact, since a striking result would not be posted otherwise.
Assume it is contaminated and dismiss it without any further checking.
As a claim to check: note the source type, look for reproduction, and ask what it did not measure.
Ignore it forever, because un-reproduced results never turn out to matter.
quiz.explain: Source discipline weights a claim by evidence, not by how striking it sounds.

exercise.prompt: A striking, un-reproduced preprint is trending on social media. How should you treat it?
choices: As established fact, since a result trending this widely must already have been vetted carefully by the crowd.
As a claim to check: note the source type, look for reproduction, and ask what it did not measure.
Ignore it permanently, because anything on social media is automatically wrong.
Assume it is contaminated and move on without looking at the actual method.
exercise.explain: Source discipline weights a claim by evidence and reproduction, not by how striking it sounds.

outcome: You can navigate the literature with a canon-first, source-disciplined reading habit.

title: Beyond text: multimodal, MoE, and distillation
summary: Frontier systems move past dense text-only transformers with multimodal inputs, sparse mixture-of-experts, and distillation.
body_html:
The dense, text-only transformer is the starting point, not the whole frontier. Many multimodal models map text, images, and audio into a shared representation space, often through modality-specific encoders first, so one model can describe an image, read a chart, or answer questions that mix words and pictures. Architectures vary; the shared-space idea is the common thread, not a universal rule. The same attention machinery applies; the inputs are just encoded into a common space first.

Mixture-of-experts (MoE) changes how capacity scales. Instead of running every parameter on every token, a router sends each token to a few specialized expert sub-networks. The model can hold very many parameters while activating only a fraction per token, buying capacity without paying full compute for every token. The tradeoff is routing complexity and memory.

Distillation goes the other way, toward efficiency. A smaller student model is trained to mimic a larger teacher, capturing much of its behavior at a fraction of the serving cost. Each of these is a lever with a tradeoff: multimodality broadens inputs, MoE scales capacity per unit compute, and distillation trades a little quality for cheaper deployment. Frontier systems mix and match them.
key_points: Multimodal models share one representation space across text, images, and audio.
Mixture-of-experts routes each token to a few experts, scaling parameters without full per-token compute.
Distillation trains a smaller student to mimic a larger teacher for cheaper serving.
Each is a lever with a tradeoff; frontier systems combine them.

links.title: Learning Transferable Visual Models (CLIP)
links.note: A foundational multimodal text-image model.

links.title: Switch Transformers (Mixture-of-Experts)
links.note: Scaling parameters with sparse expert routing.

links.title: Distilling the Knowledge in a Neural Network
links.note: The original knowledge-distillation method.

flashcards.front: What does a multimodal model share across modalities?
flashcards.back: A common representation space, so one model handles text, images, and audio together.
Routes each token to a few expert sub-networks, scaling parameters without full per-token compute.
Training a smaller student to mimic a larger teacher for cheaper serving at some quality cost.

quiz.q: A model has 100B parameters but activates only about 13B per token. What architecture is this?
choices: A distilled student model trained to copy a bigger one.
A multimodal encoder that mixes text and image inputs.
A dense transformer that uses all weights every token.
A mixture-of-experts model with sparse routing.
quiz.explain: Activating a fraction of parameters per token via routing is the defining trait of MoE.

quiz.q: You need to cut serving cost while keeping most of a large model's behavior. Which lever fits?
choices: Distillation into a smaller student.
Switching to a much larger context window.
Raising the sampling temperature a little.
Adding a second multimodal input stream.
quiz.explain: Distillation trades a little quality for much cheaper deployment.

exercise.prompt: You must answer questions that combine a paragraph of text and a bar chart image. What kind of model do you need?
choices: A multimodal model that shares a representation across text and images.
A mixture-of-experts router that picks an expert per token.
A distilled student of a text-only model, run at low precision to keep the cost down.
A text-only transformer with a much larger context window.
exercise.explain: Mixed text-and-image input is best handled by a multimodal model with a shared representation space.

outcome: You can describe multimodal models, mixture-of-experts, and distillation and their tradeoffs.

title: Efficient adaptation: LoRA, PEFT, and quantization
summary: You rarely need to retrain a whole model. Parameter-efficient methods and quantization let you adapt and run large models on modest hardware.
body_html:
Full fine-tuning updates every weight in a model, which for a large model means enormous memory and a fresh multi-gigabyte copy per task. Parameter-efficient fine-tuning (PEFT) avoids that by freezing the pretrained weights and training a small number of new parameters instead. The dominant method, LoRA (low-rank adaptation), inserts small pairs of low-rank matrices alongside selected weight matrices and trains only those. Because the update is constrained to a low-rank form, the trainable parameter count drops by orders of magnitude while quality on the target task stays close to full fine-tuning. The frozen base is shared, so each task is just a small adapter you can swap in.

Quantization attacks a different cost: the precision of the numbers themselves. Weights are usually stored in 16-bit floats; quantizing them to 8-bit or 4-bit integers reliably shrinks memory and, with suitable kernels, can speed inference, at some risk to accuracy that careful schemes largely contain. QLoRA combines the two ideas: quantize the frozen base to 4-bit to fit it in limited memory, then train LoRA adapters on top in higher precision. That combination is what lets people adapt very large models on a single consumer GPU.

The practical takeaway is a decision tree. If you need new behavior and have task data, reach for LoRA or another PEFT method before full fine-tuning, since it is cheaper, faster, and gives you swappable adapters. If memory or latency at inference is the bottleneck, quantize. And remember that for many tasks you need neither: prompting, retrieval, or tool use adapts behavior with no training at all, and finetuning is the move only when those plateau.
key_points: PEFT freezes the base model and trains a small set of new parameters; LoRA is the dominant method.
LoRA adds low-rank adapter matrices to selected layers, cutting trainable parameters by orders of magnitude.
Quantization stores weights at lower precision (8-bit, 4-bit) to save memory and speed inference.
QLoRA quantizes the frozen base and trains LoRA adapters on top; prefer prompting or retrieval before any finetuning.

links.title: LoRA: Low-Rank Adaptation of Large Language Models
links.note: The low-rank adaptation method that made PEFT mainstream.

links.title: QLoRA: Efficient Finetuning of Quantized LLMs
links.note: Quantize the frozen base to 4-bit, train LoRA adapters on top.

links.title: Hugging Face PEFT documentation
links.note: Practical library for LoRA and other parameter-efficient methods.

flashcards.front: What does LoRA train, and what does it leave frozen?
flashcards.back: It freezes the pretrained weights and trains small low-rank adapter matrices added to selected layers, cutting trainable parameters dramatically.

flashcards.front: What does quantization trade, and for what?
flashcards.back: It trades some numeric precision (16-bit to 8-bit or 4-bit) for less memory and faster inference; careful schemes keep the accuracy cost small.
A 4-bit quantized frozen base (to fit in limited memory) with higher-precision LoRA adapters trained on top, enabling adaptation of large models on one GPU.

quiz.q: Compared with full fine-tuning, LoRA primarily reduces:
choices: The model's vocabulary, by pruning rare tokens out of the tokenizer first
The number of trainable parameters (and thus memory and per-task storage), by training small low-rank adapters
The context window, by compressing older tokens into shorter summaries
The need for any task data, since adapters can be trained with none at all
quiz.explain: LoRA freezes the base and trains a tiny low-rank update, slashing trainable parameters and giving swappable per-task adapters.

quiz.q: You need to run a large model but it does not fit in your GPU memory. The most direct lever is:
choices: Switch to a much larger context window so the whole model has room to load
Increase the sampling temperature so it uses less memory
Quantize the weights to lower precision (8-bit or 4-bit)
Add more LoRA adapters until the weights compress down
quiz.explain: Quantization shrinks the memory footprint of the weights directly. LoRA addresses training cost, not base-model inference memory.

quiz.q: Before reaching for any finetuning to change behavior, you should first consider:
choices: Quantizing the weights to 1-bit so the model fits in far less memory
Retraining the entire model from scratch on a large dataset built for your specific task
Buying more GPUs so you can afford a longer fine-tuning run
Whether prompting, retrieval, or tool use already gets you there with no training
quiz.explain: Often no training is needed: prompting, retrieval, and tools adapt behavior for free. Finetune only when those plateau.

exercise.prompt: Put the steps of a QLoRA-style efficient adaptation in order.
items: Start from a pretrained base model and keep its weights frozen
Quantize the frozen base to low precision (for example 4-bit) to fit it in limited memory
Attach small low-rank LoRA adapter matrices to selected layers
Train only the adapter parameters on your task data
Serve by running the frozen base together with the trained adapters
exercise.explain: Freeze the base, quantize it to fit, add low-rank adapters, train only those, then serve base plus adapters. The base is shared; each task is a small adapter.

outcome: Choose between prompting, PEFT, and quantization for a real constraint, and explain what LoRA and QLoRA each change.

sources.claim: LoRA freezes pretrained weights and injects trainable low-rank matrices.
sources.title: Hu et al., LoRA (2021)

sources.claim: QLoRA finetunes adapters over a 4-bit quantized frozen base.
sources.title: Dettmers et al., QLoRA (2023)

title: Capstone: read a frontier claim like a skeptic
summary: The frontier arrives as papers and press releases. Practice the reading that separates a real result from a headline: what was measured, what was overclaimed, what evidence is missing.
body_html:
Most frontier knowledge reaches you as a claim before it reaches you as consensus, so the load-bearing skill is critical reading. A strong result names exactly what it measured, on what benchmark, against what baseline, with what uncertainty. A weak one leans on a superlative (state-of-the-art, human-level, emergent) and a single number on a benchmark the authors chose, and lets you supply the generalization in your head. The evaluating-AI track gave you the tools; this capstone applies them to a paper-shaped claim.

You are handed an abstract and its headline claim. Work the steps to separate what is actually supported from what is implied, identify the missing evidence, and reach a calibrated verdict. The reasoning follows each choice. The goal is the reflex of asking, measured how, compared to what, and would it replicate.
key_points: A credible claim states the metric, the benchmark, the baseline, and the uncertainty.
A self-selected benchmark and a superlative are signals to slow down, not to believe.
Generalization from one benchmark to a broad capability is the most common overclaim.
A calibrated verdict is promising-but-unverified until independent replication on standard tasks.

links.title: Anthropic: A statistical approach to model evals
links.note: What rigorous evaluation reporting looks like; use it as the bar to judge claims against.

flashcards.front: Four things a credible empirical claim names.
flashcards.back: The metric, the benchmark, the baseline it beats, and the uncertainty (interval or significance).

flashcards.front: What is the most common overclaim in frontier abstracts?
flashcards.back: Generalizing from one self-selected benchmark to a broad capability (for example calling a single-benchmark gain 'better reasoning').

quiz.q: An abstract reports a 40% gain on a benchmark the authors introduced, with no baseline comparison. Your first read is:
choices: Treat it as unverified: a self-made benchmark with no baseline does not establish a general result
The method is useless, since a missing baseline means the work is worthless
The 40% number on its own proves the capability the authors claim
State-of-the-art is confirmed, since the gain over prior work is so large
quiz.explain: A self-selected benchmark with no baseline is the weakest form of evidence. It is a hypothesis to test, not a result to cite.

quiz.q: The strongest single thing that would raise your confidence in the claim is:
choices: A larger, more confident font and clearer formatting in the abstract
Independent replication on standard, widely used benchmarks with a baseline and reported uncertainty
More authors on the paper, which signals a more careful experiment
A press release from the lab announcing the result to journalists
quiz.explain: Independent replication on shared benchmarks, with a baseline and uncertainty, is what turns a claim into a result.

exercise.prompt: Judge the frontier claim one step at a time.
exercise.scenario_html:
Abstract (excerpt): "We present Method X, which improves reasoning by 40% and achieves state-of-the-art performance. On our new ReasonBench suite, Method X scores 0.82. These results demonstrate a step change in machine reasoning."

Headline: "New method gives AI human-level reasoning."

The abstract reports no baseline number, no comparison to existing methods, no confidence interval, and ReasonBench was introduced in the same paper.

choices: A score of 0.82 on ReasonBench, a benchmark the authors introduced in this paper
Performance measured across all of the standard public reasoning benchmarks in use today
Human-level reasoning ability, demonstrated broadly across many domains
Nothing measurable at all, since the paper reports no concrete numbers
steps.explain: The only datum is 0.82 on the authors' own ReasonBench. Everything broader is interpretation layered on top of that single number.

choices: That the experiment was run on a GPU rather than on ordinary hardware
Generalizing a single self-made-benchmark score into 'reasoning improved 40%' and 'human-level reasoning'
That the paper lists several authors instead of a single named one
That the abstract uses a slightly larger font than the body text
steps.explain: The leap from one self-selected benchmark to a broad, human-level reasoning capability is the overclaim. The 40% also has no stated baseline.

steps.q: What evidence is missing to support the claim?
choices: More superlatives in the abstract to make the result sound stronger
Nothing further is needed; the claim is already complete exactly as the abstract currently states it
A baseline comparison, results on standard external benchmarks, and reported uncertainty
A longer abstract that explains the method in much greater detail
steps.explain: Without a baseline, external benchmarks, and uncertainty, there is no way to know if 0.82 is good or if the gain is real.

choices: Dismiss the method entirely, since a self-made benchmark can never tell you anything useful about real reasoning ability
Cite it as solid proof that human-level AI reasoning has now been achieved in practice
Accept it as the new state-of-the-art and build on top of the reported number
Promising but unverified: interesting method, but the reasoning claim awaits a baseline and independent replication on standard benchmarks
steps.explain: Neither hype nor dismissal. The honest position is promising-but-unverified, pending replication with baselines and uncertainty.

exercise.outcome: You separated the measured datum from the implied capability, named the missing evidence, and reached a calibrated promising-but-unverified verdict.

outcome: Read a frontier claim critically: what was measured, what was overclaimed, what evidence is missing, and a calibrated verdict.
